coredata.dc:title: Multipurpose sensing applications of biocompatible radish-derived carbon dots as Cu2+ and acetic acid vapor sensors
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Praneerad, Janjira
Thongsai, Nichaphat
Supchocksoonthorn, Preeyanuch
Kladsomboon, Sumana
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: A recent trend in the preparation of carbon dots, optically unique nanomaterials, revolves around the use of readily-available, low-cost natural resources as precursors and their multipurpose applications. In this work, a hydrothermal method for preparing biocompatible carbon dots from radish was developed. The carbon dots were then tested for sensing of Cu2+ and acetic acid vapor. The carbon dots exhibited blue emission under UV illumination with, a quantum yield of 15%. The fluorescence emission was selectively quenched when Cu2+ ions were added, giving a detection limit of 0.16 μM. A paper-based fluorescent sensor was fabricated and shown to sense Cu2+ with a limit of detection of 6.8 μM. The carbon dots were able to determine the Cu2+ concentration in real water samples, with excellent recovery and reliability. The carbon dots were also used as the sensing material in an optical electronic nose, and tested for real-time detection of acetic acid vapor. Using principal component analysis, different ratios of acetic acid to methanol in solution were successfully identified with a detection limit of 15.5%. The acetic acid concentration in a real vinegar sample was also accurately determined. Our results demonstrated that label-free carbon dots derived from readily available radish can be simply used as versatile probes, giving them potential uses in multipurpose sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Radish
Hydrothermal
Cu2+ sensor
Vapor sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Cu2+ Ion in Water Samples
Synthesis and Characterization of the Carbon Dots
Analysis of Cu2+ Ion in Real Water Samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis and applications of carbon dots.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of carbon dots, (b) XPS survey-spectrum of carbon dots, and high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C1s, (d) O1s, and (e) N1s regions.
(a) TEM image of carbon dots and (b) histogram of size distribution.

(a) UV–vis and fluorescence spectra and (b) excitation-dependence emission spectra of carbon dots. Insets show carbon dot solutions under white light and UV illumination at 365 nm. (c) Relative absorbance and fluorescence emission intensity of carbon dot solution under 365 nm irradiation and (d) relative fluorescence emission intensity of carbon dot and quinine sulfate solution over two months.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of carbon dots (CDs), (b) relative fluorescence intensity of carbon dots in the presence of metal ions, (c) fluorescence spectra of carbon dots with different concentrations of Cu2+, (d) Stern-Volmer plot, (e) fluorescence spectra, and (f) relative fluorescence intensity of carbon dots with Cu2+ and metal ion interference.

(a) Carbon dot-coated paper-based sensor after dipping into metal ion solutions, (b) plots of RGB values extracted from paper-based sensor. Insets show paper-based sensors at different concentrations of Cu2+ as a linear plot.
Percentage recovery of Cu2+ in water samples and sum of RGB values obtained from paper-based sensor.

(a) Representative diagram of optical electronic nose, (b) sensor responses to acetic acid (AcOH) solutions and vinegar sample, (c) PCA score plots for acetic acid solutions and vinegar sample, (d) sensor response to acetic acid/methanol (MeOH) vapor, (e) PCA score plots for acetic acid/methanol vapor, and (f) plot of sensor response (%) against acetic acid concentration.

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectra of carbon dots in the absence and presence of acetic acid vapor and (b) schematic representation of strong optical absorption of carbon dots in the presence of acetic acid.
Percentage of cell survival from MTT assay experiment.
Performance of different fluorescent probes in Cu2+ detection.
Adsorption capacities of different adsorbents for Cu2+ detection.

row.entry: Carbon nanotubes immobilized by calcium alginate (CNTs/CA)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Percentage recovery of spiked Cu2+ ions using carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Multipurpose sensing applications of biocompatible radish-derived carbon dots as Cu2+ and acetic acid vapor sensors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathum Thani 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiological Technology, Faculty of Medical Technology, Mahidol University, Nakhon Pathom 73170, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A recent trend in the preparation of carbon dots, optically unique nanomaterials, revolves around the use of readily-available, low-cost natural resources as precursors and their multipurpose applications. In this work, a hydrothermal method for preparing biocompatible carbon dots from radish was developed. The carbon dots were then tested for sensing of Cu2+ and acetic acid vapor. The carbon dots exhibited blue emission under UV illumination with, a quantum yield of 15%. The fluorescence emission was selectively quenched when Cu2+ ions were added, giving a detection limit of 0.16 μM. A paper-based fluorescent sensor was fabricated and shown to sense Cu2+ with a limit of detection of 6.8 μM. The carbon dots were able to determine the Cu2+ concentration in real water samples, with excellent recovery and reliability. The carbon dots were also used as the sensing material in an optical electronic nose, and tested for real-time detection of acetic acid vapor. Using principal component analysis, different ratios of acetic acid to methanol in solution were successfully identified with a detection limit of 15.5%. The acetic acid concentration in a real vinegar sample was also accurately determined. Our results demonstrated that label-free carbon dots derived from readily available radish can be simply used as versatile probes, giving them potential uses in multipurpose sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots, also known as carbon nanodots or carbon quantum dots, are optically unique and stable nanomaterials [1]. They are carbon-based, and can therefore be synthesized from any precursor that contains carbon atoms, preferably with a high carbon content. They are commonly produced using thermal treatments, including hydrothermal and solvothermal techniques or pyrolysis, usually with the addition of an acid catalyst. Under these conditions, the precursors undergo dehydration, oxidation, and carbonization reactions. This results in the formation of hydrophilic functional groups, including hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylate groups, on the surface of the carbon dot core [2]. These functional groups are exceptional chelating ligands or binding sites for small molecules and metal ions. Such binding can modulate the optical properties of the carbon dots, allowing their use as sensors. Furthermore, due to their nanometer size and high surface area, carbon dots are excellent chemical and bio-probes with high selectivity and sensitivity, and have a variety of sensing applications [3,4].

In this work, water-soluble, fluorescent carbon dots were prepared from local white radish using a one-step hydrothermal process in the presence of sulfuric acid. Recent trends in carbon dot preparation are toward the use of natural precursors. Radish is readily available and relatively cheap, and is thus an ideal choice as precursor. It is also rich in carbohydrates, proteins, vitamins, and minerals, making it suitable for the production of carbon dots at high yields and quantum efficiencies. In addition to strong fluorescence emission, the carbon dots possessed strong UV absorption tailing into the visible region. In this study, we made use of both optical emission and absorption for sensing copper (II) ions (Cu2+) and acetic acid vapor. Metal ion pollution has become a serious issue, inducing severe risks to organisms and the environment, and impacting human health. The Cu2+ ion is involved in metabolic and enzymatic processes, including the production of cytochrome C oxidase, superoxide dismutase, and tyrosinase [5]. However, exposure to high levels of Cu2+ can cause renal disorders, and damage to the nervous system, liver, and kidney. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency sets a maximum contaminant level for Cu2+ at 1.3 mg L−1 (20 μM) [6]. There is therefore a demand for easy-to-fabricate, sensitive sensors for Cu2+ detection. A number of analytical methods have been reported for the detection of Cu2+ ions, including high performance liquid chromatography [7], mass spectroscopy [8], atomic absorption spectroscopy [9], ion chromatography [10], flame atomic absorption spectroscopy [11], coupled plasma spectroscopy [12], electron paramagnetic resonance [13], capillary electrophoresis [14]. The major drawbacks of these techniques are the tedious sample treatment procedures and the need for sophisticated and large instrumentation. Fluorescence-based sensors offer distinct advantages over other metal ion detection methods because of their high sensitivity and selectivity, and low cost. They can also be used to fabricate fluorescent colorimetric paper-based devices which are cheap, simple, and portable.

In addition to Cu2+ sensing, we integrated the radish-derived carbon dots into an optical electronic nose and used this to sense acetic acid vapor at room temperature. The electronic nose is an electronic device that mimics the human nose and can detect odors, gases, and vapors. They are widely used in safety and quality control in the food, pharmaceutical, clinical, and chemical industries [15–17]. Different types of electronic nose can detect, for example, electrical, optical, and mass transductions [18]. The strong optical absorption properties, polar functional groups, and high surface area of carbon dots make them a promising material for use as a sensing layer for the real-time detection of gases and vapor by an optical electronic nose. Despite their widespread use in sensing applications in solid phase and solutions, carbon dots have been little used in vapor sensing, though Dolai et al. reported the fabrication of a carbon-dot aerogel sensor for detection of aromatic volatile organic compounds [19]. Recently, we reported the use of yogurt- and enokitake mushroom-derived carbon dots to sense formic acid and alcohol vapors, respectively [20,21]. No studies have reported acetic acid vapor detection using carbon dots. Acetic acid is the most widely used carboxylic acid in the food, plastic, pharmaceutical, dye, and insecticide industries. It is colorless, flammable, corrosive, and volatile, with a pungent odor. Acetic acid vapor has been reported to cause erosion of teeth, and has also been associated with Salmonella contamination of packaged meat [22]. Liquid acetic acid can be detected using flow injection analysis, liquid chromatography, or gas chromatography [23]. However, sensors that can detect acetic acid vapor are rare. Those that have been reported include coated piezoelectric crystal sensors [24], optical fiber sensors [25], and thin-film oxide sensors [26]. While offering high sensitivity, these sensors are complex to fabricate and must normally be operated at high temperatures. A carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose thus offers an alternative choice as a real-time, label-free, and room-temperature acetic acid vapor sensor. In addition to fluorescence emission, the utilization of their optical absorption properties allows carbon dots to be used in a much wider range of applications.

Herein, we developed a one-step hydrothermal method for producing the carbon dots from radish. The photostability of the carbon dots was investigated under several conditions. The as-prepared carbon dots showed high quantum yields and could be used as Cu2+ and acetic acid vapor sensors. An easy-to-fabricate, portable, paper-based fluorescent sensor was also prepared for the detection of Cu2+. The carbon dots were used to determine the concentration of Cu2+ in real water samples and of acetic acid in a real vinegar sample. The biocompatibility of the carbon dots was tested to confirm their potential use in biomedical applications. Our results demonstrated that carbon dots obtained from radish can be used as sensing probes without further modification and have a range of promising chemical sensing and biomedical applications.

All chemicals were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Radish and vinegar were purchased from a local supermarket. High-grade regenerated cellulose dialysis membrane (1000 Da MWCO) was bought from Membrane Filtration Product, Inc. Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments.

Fresh radish was washed thoroughly with water, cut into small pieces, dried under sunlight and ground using a blender. The dried radish (3.0 g) was dissolved in a 0.5 M sulfuric acid solution (50 mL). The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 7 h. After the reaction completed, the autoclave was gradually cooled to room temperature. The solution was passed through filter paper, neutralized with 10 M NaOH, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant liquid was then purified using a dialysis membrane (1000 Da MWCO) for 72 h to remove unreacted reactants and non-fluorescent large particles. Finally, the solution was freeze dried to yield carbon dots as a brown solid powder (0.2741 g, 9.14%).

The FT-IR spectrum of the carbon dots was measured using an FT-IR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Spectrum 2000) and KBr pellets. The elemental presence and composition of the carbon dots were investigated using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos Analytical, Manchester, UK). The UV–vis and fluorescence emission spectra of the carbon dot aqueous solution (0.02 g L−1) were measured using a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, UV-1700 PharmaSpec) and fluorescence spectrometer (Jasco, FP-6200), respectively. TEM images were captured using a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, at a magnification of 100,000×. The zeta potential of the carbon dot solutions (0.01 g L−1) was measured using a Nano Particle Analyzer (HORIBA, Nano Partica SZ-100) at 25 °C with a scattering angle of 173°. The refractive index, viscosity, pH, and conductivity of the solution were 1.33, 0.894 mPa·s, 12, and 31.327 mS cm−1, respectively.

The quantum yields of the carbon dots were measured using the following equation [3,27–29]:
Φ x
=
Φ R
m x
m R
n x 2
n R 2
where Φx is the quantum yield of carbon dots, m is the slope of the curve between the integrated fluorescence emission intensity measured using a fluorescence spectrometer and the absorbance measured using a UV–vis spectrometer and n is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript X identifies the carbon dots. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was used as a reference (ΦR = 54% at 360 nm), and is identified by subscript R.

A carbon dot aqueous solution (0.1 g L−1) was incubated under continuous UV irradiation (365 nm, 30 W lamp) and ambient conditions. The fluorescence emission intensities and absorbance were recorded as a function of time using a fluorescence spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm and a UV–vis spectrometer, respectively.

The selectivity of the carbon dots toward the following metal ions was studied: Pb2+ (Pb(NO3)2), Cu2+(CuSO4·5H2O), Na+ (NaNO3), Fe2+ (Fe(NH4)2SO4), Cr3+ (Cr(NO3)3), Zn2+ (ZnCl2), Mg2+ (MgCl2), K+ (KNO3), Co2+ (CoCl2), Ni2+ (Ni(NO3)2), Sn2+ (SnCl2), and Cd2+ (Cd(NO3)2·6H2O). Aqueous solutions of carbon dots (3 mL, 0.1 g L−1) and metal ions (2 mL, 100 μM) were mixed and incubated for 30 min at room temperature. All mixtures were transferred to a quartz cuvette and their fluorescence emission intensities were recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

The sensitivity of the carbon dots toward Cu2+ ions was measured. Cu2+ aqueous solutions with concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 μM were added to the carbon dot solution (4 mL, 0.1 g L−1). All samples were allowed to incubate for 30 min at room temperature and their fluorescence emission intensities were recorded as above.

To further compare the selectivity of the carbon dots toward Cu2+ in the presence of other metal ions, a carbon dot aqueous solution (3 mL, 0.1 g L−1) was mixed with a Cu2+ solution (1 mL, 100 μM), with other metal ion solutions as interference (1 mL, 100 μM). The mixtures were incubated for 30 min at room temperature, and their fluorescence emission intensities were recorded as above.

Whatman No.1 filter papers (2 × 2 cm2) were immersed in a carbon dot aqueous solution (10 g L−1) and dried at 60 °C. The carbon dot-coated papers were dipped in the following metal ion solutions (100 μM): Pb2+ (Pb(NO3)2), Cu2+(CuSO4·5H2O), Na+ (NaNO3), Fe2+ (Fe(NH4)2SO4), Cr3+ (Cr(NO3)3), Zn2+ (ZnCl2), Mg2+ (MgCl2), K+ (KNO3), Co2+ (CoCl2), Ni2+ (Ni(NO3)2), Sn2+ (SnCl2), and Cd2+ (Cd(NO3)2·6H2O). They were then dried at 60 °C. The paper-based sensors were illuminated under a UV lamp (365 nm) and pictures were taken using an IPhone 6 camera at default settings.

The sensitivity of the carbon dot-loaded filter paper was calculated from the red-green-blue (RGB) values using the following equations (n = 7):
Sum
RGB
values = Red + Green + Blue

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Cu2+ Ion in Water Samples
ce:section.ce:para: Five water samples were taken for Cu2+ analysis: tap water, river water, and three different bottled waters. These were passed through filter paper (Whatman No.1, 150 mm pore-size membrane). Carbon dot aqueous solutions (4 mL, 0.1 g L−1) were prepared using those water samples and spiked with Cu2+ (5 μM). These were incubated for 30 min at room temperature and their fluorescence emission intensities were analyzed using a fluorescence spectrometer and the paper-based sensors. The paper-based sensor was fabricated similarly.

The recovery, relative standard deviation, and relative RGB values as a percentage were calculated using the following equations:
% Recovery =
Concentration of
Cu
2 +
found
Concentration of
Cu
2 +
added
× 100
% RSD =
SD
X ¯
× 100
where SD is the standard deviation (n = 5) and
X ¯
is the averaged concentration of Cu2+ (n = 5).
Relative
RGB
value =
RGB standard
RGB real
× 100
where RGBstandard is the summed RGB value from the standard sample (DI water) and RGBreal is the summed RGB value from the real water sample.

To investigate the acetic acid vapor sensing properties of the carbon dots, a film was prepared by drop-casting carbon dot aqueous solution (1 mL, 20 g L−1), which had been ultrasonicated for at least 30 min, on a cleaned 1 × 1 inch glass slide. The film was then allowed to dry at 60 °C overnight. An optical electronic nose system was constructed, comprising a light source, carbon dot film, a photo detector, and a data acquisition unit. Light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and a commercial photo detector (ET-TCS230) were used to measure the intensity of light passing through the film. Eight gas sensor arrays were fabricated, based on the eight colors of the LEDs: infrared, red (638 nm), yellow (587 nm), green (537 nm), blue (457 nm), blue-green (472 nm), violet (399 nm), and white [30]. The vapor sensing response of the carbon dot thin film was tested under a dynamic gas flow through a home-made chamber. Nitrogen (99.99%) at a flow rate of approximately 500 mL min−1 into the sensor chamber was used as the reference and carrier gas. A solenoid valve switched between the reference gas and the sample gases. The sample gases were prepared by flowing nitrogen gas through acetic acid solutions. All measurements were carried out at room temperature.

The biocompatibility of the carbon dots was tested using an MTT assay. MCF-7 breast cancer cell lines were cultured in 96 well plates at a density of 2 × 104 cells per well. The cell lines were treated for 72 h in media with four different concentrations of carbon dots: 10, 20, 40, and 80 μg mL−1. The cells were then washed with 1× PBS and incubated in 100 μL of 0.5 g L−1 3-(4,5‑dimethylthiazol‑2‑yl)‑2,5‑diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) at 37 °C for 30 min. Under light protection, the dark blue crystals of formazan (MTT metabolites) were dissolved with 100 μL dimethyl sulfoxide and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Absorbance was measured at 570 and 650 nm, using a microplate reader spectrometer. Viable cells were represented as those that survived, and the percentage was calculated using the following equation (n = 3).
Survival
%
=
As 570
−
As 650
Ac 570
−
Ac 650
× 100 ,
where As570 and As650 are the absorbance of the test samples at 570 and 650 nm, and Ac570 and Ac650 those of the controls [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and Characterization of the Carbon Dots
ce:section.ce:para: Radish is a common vegetable that is rich in carbohydrates, proteins, vitamins, and minerals. These nutritious compounds comprise carbon-based molecules and polymers with various heteroatoms. Radish is therefore an excellent source of the carbon needed for the synthesis of carbon dots and the heteroatoms necessary to achieve high photoluminescence efficiency. The hydrothermal method is simple and is widely employed for synthesizing carbon dots from natural raw materials [32], usually with the aid of an acid catalyst/dehydrating agent. In this study, carbon dots were prepared using a one-pot hydrothermal method. In the presence of sulfuric acid, radish was carbonized at 200 °C for 7 h. After purification by filtering, centrifuging, dialysis, and freeze-drying, brown carbon dots were obtained as a powder at a moderate mass yield of 9%. The as-prepared carbon dots were then tested as sensors for Cu2+ and acetic acid vapor. The process is shown as Fig. 1 .

FT-IR spectroscopy was first used to characterize the functional groups of the carbon dots. A broad signal of NH/OH stretching vibrational modes was observed at 3403 cm−1, while the small signal at 2934 cm−1 was attributed to CH stretching mode (Fig. 2a). Signals at 1631 and 1113 cm−1 were attributed to the carbonyl/vinyl (CO/CC) and CC stretching modes, respectively. These vibrational modes suggested that the carbon dots included oxygen-containing functional groups, making them highly water soluble. The formation of these groups is known to arise from oxidation reaction of the carbon-based molecules and polymers in radish in the presence of strong acid and at high temperature and pressure.

The elemental presence and composition of the carbon dots were then investigated using XPS, a surface specific technique routinely used to characterize the surface of materials to a penetration depth of approximately 10 nm [33]. The survey XPS spectrum showed signals at 284.8, 399.8, and 531.8 eV, which were attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, at atomic concentrations of 62.23%, 26.17%, and 5.61% (Fig. 2b). The presence of nitrogen was attributed to the proteins, amino acids, and vitamins in the radish. The small signal of sulfur was due to the use of sulfuric acid during synthesis. The carbon dots were therefore highly nitrogen-doped and lightly sulfur-doped. The high-resolution C1s spectrum exhibited the deconvoluted signals of CC, CC, CO, and CO at 283.7, 285.0, 286.5, and 288.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 2c). The high-resolution O1s spectrum showed the presence of CO/SiO, CO, COH/COC, and OCO at 503.1, 531.5, 532.5, and 533.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 2d). The high-resolution N1s spectrum exhibited the characteristic signals of pyrrolinic and pyridinic nitrogens at 400.2 and 398.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 2e). Both the FT-IR and XPS results confirmed the presence of hydrophilic oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups. These groups are reported to be responsible for the photoluminescence properties of carbon dots and the presence of binding sites with small molecules and ions, providing unique sensing capability [1,34,35].

The TEM technique was used to characterize the morphology and particle size of the carbon dots, which were shown to be spherical in shape and largely aggregated (Fig. 3a). Such aggregation is typically observed on carbon dots because of the strong polar-polar interaction between the carbon dots through hydrophilic functional groups on the surface [36]. The corresponding particle size distribution histograms suggested that these carbon dots had diameters ranging from 2 to 7 nm and an average diameter of 4.4 nm (Fig. 3b). The zeta potential of the carbon dots was determined to be −1 mV, indicating instability or rapid aggregation. This was consistent with the particle aggregation observed in the TEM image.

To explore the optical properties, an aqueous solution of carbon dots was prepared and characterized using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. From the UV–vis spectrum, the carbon dots exhibited strong optical absorption in the UV region (200–400 nm) tailing into the visible region (up to 600 nm). Absorption shoulders at 260 nm and 330 nm were observed, corresponding to π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 carbons and n-π* transition of the carbonyl group, respectively (Fig. 4a). The fluorescence spectrum of the carbon dots showed the strongest fluorescence emission at 450 nm when using an excitation wavelength of 360 nm with a broad range from 400 nm to 600 nm. The fluorescence emission maximums also shifted to a higher wavelength as the excitation wavelength (320–480 nm) increased, indicating that the fluorescence emission of the carbon dots was excitation-dependent [37] (Fig. 4b). The broad emission and excitation-dependence are known to arise from the different sizes of the carbon dots and emissive sites/functional groups on the surface [38]. The solution showed blue emission under UV at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm while appearing completely brown and transparent under daylight. This is consistent with previous reports that carbon dots with a size of approximately 5 nm are strong blue emitters under UV excitation [39]. The quantum yield was calculated using quinine sulfate as a reference. We found that the as-prepared carbon dots had a relative quantum yield of up to 15%, which is high for unmodified or non-passivated carbon dots prepared from natural precursors [40,41] and slightly higher than that of the carbon dots prepared from rose-heart radish [28]. This moderately high quantum yield is possibly due to nitrogen and sulfur doping of the carbon dots, as indicated by the XPS results. The presence of these heteroatoms is known to facilitate the radiative recombination process through efficient trapping of electrons [42].

The physical/chemical stability of carbon dots is of great interest in practical applications. The photostability of the carbon dots under ambient conditions and prolonged UV illumination was therefore investigated (Fig. 4c and d). Under continuous UV irradiation for 6 h (365 nm, 30 W lamp), the absorbance did not significantly change, whereas the emission intensity remained above 85%. When stored under ambient conditions for eight weeks, the emission intensity did not significantly change, suggesting stability comparable with that of a conventional fluorescent dye like quinine sulfate and carbon dots from other natural sources [43,44]. Neither detrimental photobleaching nor discoloring were visible to the naked eyes. This suggested that the radish-derived carbon dots are suitable fluorophores for applications that require photostability.

Carbon dots are regarded as unique chemical sensing probes because of their surface-sensitive optical properties and presence of the hydrophilic functional groups including the hydroxyl, amino, and carboxylate groups. They are therefore excellent chelating ligands for metal ion complexation and have strong interaction with polar molecules. The optical properties of carbon dots are sensitive to the adsorption or interaction with these groups, making them attractive for use as sensors. In this study, we used both the optical absorption and emission properties of carbon dots as sensing probes. First, the fluorescence emission properties were employed for trace analysis of metal ions. Pollution by metal ions has become a critical issue as they pose severe risks to organisms, health, and the environment. The selectivity of carbon dots toward metal ions was determined using Pb2+, Cu2+, Na+, Fe2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Mg2+, K+, Co2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, and Cd2+. To evaluate the selectivity of the carbon dots, we mixed metal ion and carbon dot solutions and measured the fluorescence intensity. The largest quenching of fluorescence intensity at 360 nm was found for Cu2+, at 55% (Fig. 5a and b). The other ions produced only slight decreases in florescence. The carbon dots maintained excellent selectivity toward Cu2+ as the concentration of other metal ions was increased tenfold. This confirmed the strong selectivity of our carbon dots to Cu2+ ions. The selective quenching by Cu2+ was attributed to the carboxyl-rich carbon dots having higher thermodynamic affinity and electron-donating ability, and to faster chelating between the carboxyl groups and Cu2+, leading to strong complex interaction between the carbon dots and Cu2+ [5,32,45–47]. From our previous report, the strong coordination between Cu2+ and carbon dots may change the complex structure from octahedral to square planar configurations [48]. In addition, the favorable energy level of the d-orbitals of Cu2+ with respect to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals of the carbon dots produces non-radiative photoinduced electron transfer, and consequently fluorescence quenching [39,49].

Copper and its compounds are widely used in alloys, electronics, as catalysts, etc. Copper contamination is an inevitable result. As Cu2+ ion usually exists alongside other environmentally-relevant metal ions, the specific detection of Cu2+ in the presence of such interferences was evaluated. The experiment was carried out by measuring the fluorescence emission of the carbon dots in the presence of Cu2+ mixed with other metal ions (Fig. 5e). We found that the fluorescence emission of the carbon dots in the presence of interference contained to exhibit Cu2+ quenching of approximately 35–55%, comparable with the Cu2+ sample without interference (Fig. 5f). This confirmed that the selectivity of carbon dots toward Cu2+ remained excellent, even when other metal ions were present.

Having demonstrated that our radish-derived carbon dots provided excellent fluorescent probes for the detection and quantification of Cu2+ in solution, we next fabricated paper-based, solid-state fluorescent sensors as these are portable and easy-to-use. When tested by immersion in solutions of Cu2+, Na+, K+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Mg2+, and Fe3+, the carbon dot-loaded filter paper showed significant quenching by Cu2+, producing a dark blue image, whereas the other ions produced light blue images (Fig. 6a). The sensitivity of the carbon dot-loaded filter paper was then measured by immersion in different concentrations of Cu2+ and extraction of the red-green-blue (RGB) values using ImageJ software. The sum of the RGB values showed a linear dependence on the Cu2+ concentration between 10 and 60 μM with a detection limit of 6.8 μM, calculated from the 3σ/slope (Fig. 6b). The detection limit of our sensors is sufficient for detection of Cu2+ at the threshold level (20 μM) set by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) standard. To the best of our knowledge, there have been few reports of paper-based sensors fabricated using carbon dots for Cu2+ detection. Shi et al. reported the fabrication of paper-based sensors using oolong tea-derived carbon dots [57]. Their paper-based sensor could detect Cu2+ at concentration as low as 75 μM, which is above the EPA threshold. C. Liu et al. prepared red and blue carbon dots from p-phenylenediamine and sodium citrate/ammonium hydrogen carbonate [58]. Despite showing excellent sensitivity toward Cu2+ concentration in the nanomolar regime, both types of carbon dot had to be used together to fabricate the ratiometric fluorescent test papers. In comparison, the pristine, label-free carbon dots from radish developed in this work are much simpler to prepare and make excellent portable, low-cost, paper-based sensors with sufficient sensitivity for use in practical Cu2+ sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Cu2+ Ion in Real Water Samples

ce:section.ce:para: As shown above, the carbon dots were shown to be excellent probes for Cu2+ detection, as their unique fluorescence and chelating properties provided selective Cu2+ ion sensing. Next, we employed the carbon dots for sensing of acetic acid vapor, by monitoring the change in optical transmission through a carbon dot film using an optical electronic nose. Optical absorption and transmission of a material typically depend on the surrounding environment and polarity. In this experiment, a carbon dot film was prepared on a glass slide using the drop-casting method. Our optical electronic nose system also contained light sources, a photodetector, a gas flow controller, and a data acquisition unit (Fig. 8a). Aqueous solutions of acetic acid (5 and 10% v/v) were used as the vapor sources. The sensor responses corresponded to the variation in the intensity of light transmitted through the carbon dot film, measured as a function of time and gas flow, when switching between nitrogen gas and acetic acid vapor (Fig. 8b). When the acetic acid vapor was flowed through the thin film, the light transmission increased rapidly. However, transmission dropped when the nitrogen reference gas was flowed. This suggested that the carbon dots successfully sensed the acetic acid vapor. The flow of nitrogen and acetic acid were repeatedly cycled to confirm that the film was stable and could be reused multiple times. The recyclability of the carbon dots was excellent, as one carbon dot film was used for at least tens of cycles without observable deterioration. The 95% response time, or the time required for the sensing response to increase by 95%, was approximately 70 ± 4 s for the acetic acid solution, showing the fast response and recovery of our sensor. We used principal component analysis (PCA) to reduce the dimensions of the data into linearly uncorrelated variables (principal components). This was done using the orthogonal transformation method and a graphic output was obtained [59]. The sensor responses from the eight LEDs and four vapor sources were used as the input data sets. A circle marks the exclusive zone on the score plot of each solution. The first two principal components confirmed that the carbon dots were able to differentiate clearly between the two concentrations of acetic acid (5% and 10% v/v) and to distinguish both from water. Minimal overlap was found in the data points, which accounted for 91.3% of the total variability (Fig. 8c). We further tested the ability of the carbon dots to characterize a real vinegar sample (5% v/v acetic acid, as specified on the label) by measuring the sensor response in the presence of vapor from the vinegar. The PCA score plot of the real vinegar sample was strongly overlapped with that of the 5% acetic acid standard solution, confirming the ability of the carbon dots to accurately determine the acetic acid concentration of a real sample. The results also confirmed the ability of our electronic nose to sense water vapor, giving it potential uses in humidity sensing.

One of the most widely used industrial processes for production of acetic acid is via the methanol carbonylation reaction with methanol as a precursor. We further tested the sensitivity of the carbon dot-integrated electronic nose toward acetic acid-methanol mixtures (0, 25, 50, 75, and 100% v/v). The light transmitted to the film increased dramatically when the flow was switched to acetic acid-methanol vapor (Fig. 8d), then dropped when the nitrogen reference gas was flowed. The magnitude of sensor response was higher when the concentration of methanol in the mixture was higher. This may be due to the greater volatility of methanol than acetic acid, increasing the proportion of methanol in the vapor (Fig. 8e). The sensor responses were subjected to PCA. The PCA score plots of mixtures with different ratios of methanol to acetic acid showed minimal overlapping, with a total variability of 99.5%, although they are both highly volatile and have similar polarities (Fig. 8f). This indicated that our carbon dot-integrated electronic nose can be used to qualitatively and quantitatively analyze mixtures of acetic acid and methanol. The sensor response (%) of the film to acetic acid and methanol was further quantified using (IR − Ivapor) / IR × 100, where Ivapor is the sensor response to the vapor and IR is the sensor response to the reference gas. A Stern-Volmer-like plot between sensor response and acetic acid concentration in percentage was constructed. The limit of detection, determined from 3σ/slope, was 15.5%. Although this sensitivity will need improvement, the study demonstrated that our carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose can be used for real-time tracking of product yield during the methanol carbonylation process, or determination of the ratio of acetic acid to methanol. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to report the acetic acid vapor sensing properties of carbon dots and their ability to differentiate between acetic acid and methanol vapors.

Since our optical electronic nose operates by monitoring the change of light absorption/transmission through a carbon dot film, the sensitivity toward acetic acid was further investigated using UV–vis spectroscopy. A carbon dot film was prepared on a quartz slide and its absorbance was measured in the absence and presence of acetic acid vapor. A decrease in absorbance was observed when the acetic acid vapor was flowed through the film (Fig. 9a), indicating that the film absorbed less light when in the presence of acetic acid vapor. This was consistent with the electronic nose results, in which light transmission increased when acetic acid vapor was flowed. The decrease in absorbance upon exposure to acetic acid vapor is possibly due to polar-polar interactions between the acetic acid and hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots (Fig. 9b). Upon adsorption of polar molecules on the surface of carbon dots, the polarization/dipole moment around the carbon dots is modulated, leading to the change in optical absorption [29,60]. The recyclability and reversibility of the carbon dot film suggests the physisorption of acetic acid molecules on carbon dot surface. This result confirmed the change in light absorption of carbon dots upon exposure to acetic acid vapor, producing a sensing response in the optical electronic nose.

This work is the first to describe the use of carbon dots for acetic acid vapor sensing. Although the sensitivity of our carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose is currently 2–3 orders of magnitude below those reported for other acetic acid vapor sensors such as a nanoporous In2O3-based cataluminescence sensor [61], ZnO-CdS nanocomposite [62], or piezoelectric crystal covered with a diethylenetriamine film [24], our carbon dots are much simpler to prepare and our sensor can be operated at room temperature, offering much wider range of potential practical applications. Previously reported sensors need to be operated at temperatures exceeding 200–300 °C and their fabrication is more complex. Further improvements to the sensitivity of our sensors can be expected, for example by surface passivation of the carbon dots or the use of highly porous materials as composites.

Since carbon dots have potential uses as biocompatible nanomaterials, we next performed cytotoxicity testing, using MTT assay on MCF-7 breast cancer cells. A carbon dot concentration of 80 μg mL−1 was used, with an exposure of 72 h (Fig. 10 ).The carbon dots showed excellent biocompatibility, with a cell viability greater than 80% after incubation. This suggested that the carbon dots developed in this work have potential uses in biomedical applications such as cell imaging, or as biosensors and drug delivery systems.

In this work, carbon dots were prepared from radish at relatively high yields using a simple hydrothermal method. The carbon dots demonstrated their versatility and highly interactive polarity when their optical emission and absorption were used to sense Cu2+ and acetic acid vapor, showing excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward both. A paper-based fluorescent device was fabricated, and again showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity in the micromolar concentration regime. The carbon dots were shown to have practical applications, determining Cu2+ with excellent recovery rates. A carbon dot film was combined with an optical electronic nose to fabricate a real-time acetic acid vapor sensing system. Using PCA, we were able to distinguish different concentrations of acetic acid, and to accurately quantify the acetic acid concentration in a real vinegar sample. When used in the electronic nose platform, the carbon dots were able to distinguish between different ratios of acetic acid and methanol and could be reused multiple times. The carbon dots also exhibited excellent photostability and biocompatibility. Our findings suggest that carbon dots prepared from radish will find a range of applications in sensing and in biomedical devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by Thai Government Research Fund through Thammasat University Contract Number 5/2561. The authors also acknowledge the Central Scientific Instrument Center (CSIC), Thammasat University Center of Scientific Equipment for Advanced Research (TUCSEAR), Department of Chemistry, Department of Biotechnology, Faculty of Science and Technology, and Thammasat University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.11.049.
